chunks.text: " Abolition of nuclear weapons " may sound idealistic.
But in Japan, which experienced Hiroshima and Nagasaki, this is necessarily an unavoidable theme.
Today, there are about 12,500 nuclear warheads in the world.
Most are held by the United States and Russia.
Their military value is said to lie in being " unusable weapons " that exercise deterrence.
But deterrence may, like a flood, sweep everything away.
A wrong judgment, an accident, a terrorist's seize.
Once used, the civilized world ends.
Survivors of the bombs have kept telling their partly remembered stories.
Burnt skin, radiation sickness, black rain.
Things a human must not do to another human.
In 2017, the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons was adopted at the UN.
122 countries voted in favor, but all nuclear - armed states were exclude.
Japan, though the only country to have been bombed
did not join the treaty from its principal position.
This contradiction is a great issue in Japanese society.
Depending on America's nuclear umbrella while calling for abolition.
This is not an ethically desirable stance.
Nuclear weapons, once made, are hard to remove.
But humanity has banned chemical and biological weapons too.
With political will, nuclear weapons should also be abolishable.
The average age of survivors has passed eighty.
The era when we can hear living testimony will soon end.
To pass their experience accurately to the next generation is our responsibility.
The flame at Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park has continued to burn without cease.
The day that flame goes out is the day nuclear weapons are abolished.
